item.title: DACE-Det: A Synergistic Fusion Framework of Multi-Task Streams for Object Detection in Anisotropically Degraded Imagery
item.dc:creator: Tianjun Shi
Jinnan Gong
Shikai Jiang
Yu Sun
Guangzhen Bao
Pengfei Zhang
Hongyu Lu
Xiyang Zhi
Wei Zhang
item.description: Rotating Synthetic Aperture (RSA) imaging technology has the advantage of lightweight design and no requirement for on-orbit assembly. It offers an innovative solution to overcome the physical limitations of traditional large-aperture optical systems for high-resolution, high-orbit Earth observation. However, the unique, anisotropic degradation inherent to its rectangular primary mirror poses a severe challenge for automated target interpretation. Existing object detection methods lack the perceptual and adaptive capabilities to handle this directional degradation, resulting in poor performance in the RSA system. To address this, we propose a novel end-to-end Degradation-Aware and Collaboratively Enhanced Detector (DACE-Det), tailored for object detection in anisotropically degraded images. The framework first employs the Latent Degradation Pattern Embedding (LDPE) module, based on contrastive learning, to perceive and disentangle the degradation prior from the image content. The prior then guides the Hierarchical Anisotropic Feature Learning Network (HAFNet), a backbone specifically designed to extract multi-level features from anisotropically degraded images. Concurrently, the parallel restoration branch generates a detail-rich feature stream that complements the backbone features. Furthermore, the Synergistic Expert Fusion Module (SEFM) leverages a Mixture-of-Experts mechanism to dynamically fuse detection and restoration feature streams. To support research in related fields, we construct RSA-Aircraft, the first object detection dataset with anisotropic degradation, based on a full-link imaging simulation model. The dataset contains 35,704 annotated degraded images of military and civilian airports from over 100 countries and regions worldwide, captured across various years, seasons, and weather conditions. Results from both digital simulations and semi-physical experiments demonstrate that DACE-Det significantly surpasses 27 mainstream methods and outperforms the baseline by 9.86 percentage points. It offers a practical solution for object detection in RSA imagery. The RSA-Aircraft dataset will be available at https://github.com/sgtojd/RSA-Aircraft-A-benchmark-Dataset-for-Object-Detection-with-Anisotropic-Degradation for research purposes. Published: 2026-04-15T06:26:04+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.827 (must_read)

Authors: Tianjun Shi; Jinnan Gong; Shikai Jiang; Yu Sun; Guangzhen Bao; Pengfei Zhang; Hongyu Lu; Xiyang Zhi; Wei Zhang

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104382

Relevance: 0.827 (must_read)

Rotating Synthetic Aperture (RSA) imaging technology has the advantage of lightweight design and no requirement for on-orbit assembly. It offers an innovative solution to overcome the physical limitations of traditional large-aperture optical systems for high-resolution, high-orbit Earth observation. However, the unique, anisotropic degradation inherent to its rectangular primary mirror poses a severe challenge for automated target interpretation. Existing object detection methods lack the perceptual and adaptive capabilities to handle this directional degradation, resulting in poor performance in the RSA system. To address this, we propose a novel end-to-end Degradation-Aware and Collaboratively Enhanced Detector (DACE-Det), tailored for object detection in anisotropically degraded images. The framework first employs the Latent Degradation Pattern Embedding (LDPE) module, based on contrastive learning, to perceive and disentangle the degradation prior from the image content. The prior then guides the Hierarchical Anisotropic Feature Learning Network (HAFNet), a backbone specifically designed to extract multi-level features from anisotropically degraded images. Concurrently, the parallel restoration branch generates a detail-rich feature stream that complements the backbone features. Furthermore, the Synergistic Expert Fusion Module (SEFM) leverages a Mixture-of-Experts mechanism to dynamically fuse detection and restoration feature streams. To support research in related fields, we construct RSA-Aircraft, the first object detection dataset with anisotropic degradation, based on a full-link imaging simulation model. The dataset contains 35,704 annotated degraded images of military and civilian airports from over 100 countries and regions worldwide, captured across various years, seasons, and weather conditions. Results from both digital simulations and semi-physical experiments demonstrate that DACE-Det significantly surpasses 27 mainstream methods and outperforms the baseline by 9.86 percentage points. It offers a practical solution for object detection in RSA imagery. The RSA-Aircraft dataset will be available at https://github.com/sgtojd/RSA-Aircraft-A-benchmark-Dataset-for-Object-Detection-with-Anisotropic-Degradation for research purposes.

item.title: Resolution Preserving and Utilization Network for Tiny Object Detection in Large-Size Remote Sensing Imagery
item.dc:creator: Tianwei Zhang
Longfei Ren
Xu Sun
Lianru Gao
Bing Zhang
item.description: Efficient tiny object detection (TOD) in large-size remote sensing imagery (LSRSI) is particularly challenging in real-world remote sensing applications. We observe that as the input size of the remote sensing scene increases, TOD faces more severe foreground signal identification issues. To address this, we are the first to design a backbone network from the perspective of low-level spatial feature preservation and utilization, specifically for tiny object feature extraction in large-size remote sensing scene patches. The proposed architecture, referred to as the resolution preserving and utilization network (RPUN), demonstrates excellent foreground tiny object feature response identification ability when increasing the input size of remote sensing scenes, effectively maintaining detection performance comparable to that of smaller input slices. Additionally, we introduce GF2UBSv2, a large-scale panchromatic satellite imagery dataset focused on tiny urban bridge detection. Extensive experiments conducted on GF2UBSv2, DIOR, SODA-A, and DOTAv2.0 demonstrate the superior performance of RPUN compared with state-of-the-art methods. The code and dataset are available at: https: //github.com//NankleZTW. Published: 2026-04-16T19:55:44+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.826 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tianwei Zhang; Longfei Ren; Xu Sun; Lianru Gao; Bing Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3678372

Relevance: 0.826 (must_read)

Efficient tiny object detection (TOD) in large-size remote sensing imagery (LSRSI) is particularly challenging in real-world remote sensing applications. We observe that as the input size of the remote sensing scene increases, TOD faces more severe foreground signal identification issues. To address this, we are the first to design a backbone network from the perspective of low-level spatial feature preservation and utilization, specifically for tiny object feature extraction in large-size remote sensing scene patches. The proposed architecture, referred to as the resolution preserving and utilization network (RPUN), demonstrates excellent foreground tiny object feature response identification ability when increasing the input size of remote sensing scenes, effectively maintaining detection performance comparable to that of smaller input slices. Additionally, we introduce GF2UBSv2, a large-scale panchromatic satellite imagery dataset focused on tiny urban bridge detection. Extensive experiments conducted on GF2UBSv2, DIOR, SODA-A, and DOTAv2.0 demonstrate the superior performance of RPUN compared with state-of-the-art methods. The code and dataset are available at: https: //github.com//NankleZTW.

item.title: FusionMamba: Efficient 3D Point Cloud Completion via Spatially-Aware State Space Models and Multi-Scale Information Fusion
item.dc:creator: Xiang Wu
Junchao Wang
Shijie Zhang
Tianfei Chen
Xiaoping Liu
Shuyao He
item.description: Reconstructing high-fidelity geometric structures from sparse and occluded observations is a cornerstone of 3D spatial intelligence. While Transformer-based architectures have defined current benchmarks, their inherent quadratic computational complexity, O ( N 2 ), imposes a “complexity wall” that precludes real-time deployment for high-resolution synthesis. To circumvent this bottleneck, we present FusionMamba, a linear-complexity framework engineered to reconcile global structural coherence with fine-grained local geometric fidelity. Central to our architecture is the Spatially-Aware UniMamba module, which introduces a Local-Global Sequential Aggregator (LGSA) to rectify the “spatial blindness” characteristic of conventional 1D state-space models. By adaptively integrating holistic contextual dependencies with localized 3D proximity cues, the LGSA preserves topological integrity across heterogeneous scales. To counteract information decay inherent in hierarchical encoding, we further propose a Correction Module (CM). Functioning as an explicit geometric anchor, the CM employs a residual fusion mechanism to align synthesized proxies with dense spatial priors harvested directly from raw input manifolds. Extensive evaluations on the ShapeNet and PCN benchmarks, alongside zero-shot cross-domain validation on KITTI and ScanNet, demonstrate that FusionMamba establishes a new state-of-the-art ceiling. Our framework not only achieves superior restoration accuracy but also maintains a rigorous O ( N ) scaling law, facilitating the next generation of real-time intelligent perception systems. Published: 2026-04-15T08:56:07+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.814 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiang Wu; Junchao Wang; Shijie Zhang; Tianfei Chen; Xiaoping Liu; Shuyao He

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104378

Relevance: 0.814 (must_read)

Reconstructing high-fidelity geometric structures from sparse and occluded observations is a cornerstone of 3D spatial intelligence. While Transformer-based architectures have defined current benchmarks, their inherent quadratic computational complexity, O ( N 2 ), imposes a “complexity wall” that precludes real-time deployment for high-resolution synthesis. To circumvent this bottleneck, we present FusionMamba, a linear-complexity framework engineered to reconcile global structural coherence with fine-grained local geometric fidelity. Central to our architecture is the Spatially-Aware UniMamba module, which introduces a Local-Global Sequential Aggregator (LGSA) to rectify the “spatial blindness” characteristic of conventional 1D state-space models. By adaptively integrating holistic contextual dependencies with localized 3D proximity cues, the LGSA preserves topological integrity across heterogeneous scales. To counteract information decay inherent in hierarchical encoding, we further propose a Correction Module (CM). Functioning as an explicit geometric anchor, the CM employs a residual fusion mechanism to align synthesized proxies with dense spatial priors harvested directly from raw input manifolds. Extensive evaluations on the ShapeNet and PCN benchmarks, alongside zero-shot cross-domain validation on KITTI and ScanNet, demonstrate that FusionMamba establishes a new state-of-the-art ceiling. Our framework not only achieves superior restoration accuracy but also maintains a rigorous O ( N ) scaling law, facilitating the next generation of real-time intelligent perception systems.

item.title: SAR target recognition based on hierarchical azimuth aware feature enhancement network
item.dc:creator: Shichao Chen
Zhenning Dong
Ming Liu
Mingliang Tao
Yifei Fan
item.description: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) target recognition faces critical challenges due to the azimuth sensitivity of the imaging mechanism, which induces significant intra-class variance. This issue is further exacerbated under extended operating conditions (EOCs), such as noise, occlusion and depression variations etc. severely degrade recognition performance. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a hierarchical azimuth aware feature enhancement network (HAAFENet). Distinct from conventional methods, HAAFENet explicitly embeds azimuth priors into the deep learning framework to extract robust, orientation-invariant representations. Specifically, a novel azimuth aware feature enhancement module (AAFEM) is designed to dynamically generate parameters for adaptive channel and spatial modulation based on the target’s orientation. To preserve feature representativeness, a hierarchical feature fusion module (HFFM) is incorporated to integrate deep semantic features with shallow geometric details. Furthermore, we introduce two synergistic loss functions to optimize the feature space: (1) an azimuth difference feature consistency loss, which utilizes contrastive learning to enforce intra-class feature similarity across large angular variations; (2) a dynamic angular margin loss, which adjusts classification boundaries based on the equivalent number of looks (ENL) to rigorously constrain images corrupted by speckle noise. Extensive experiments on the MSTAR and SARSIM datasets demonstrate that HAAFENet achieves excellent performance, exhibiting superior robustness and discriminability under both standard and diverse extended operating condition. Published: 2026-04-15T19:56:29+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.812 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shichao Chen; Zhenning Dong; Ming Liu; Mingliang Tao; Yifei Fan

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132487

Relevance: 0.812 (must_read)

Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) target recognition faces critical challenges due to the azimuth sensitivity of the imaging mechanism, which induces significant intra-class variance. This issue is further exacerbated under extended operating conditions (EOCs), such as noise, occlusion and depression variations etc. severely degrade recognition performance. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a hierarchical azimuth aware feature enhancement network (HAAFENet). Distinct from conventional methods, HAAFENet explicitly embeds azimuth priors into the deep learning framework to extract robust, orientation-invariant representations. Specifically, a novel azimuth aware feature enhancement module (AAFEM) is designed to dynamically generate parameters for adaptive channel and spatial modulation based on the target’s orientation. To preserve feature representativeness, a hierarchical feature fusion module (HFFM) is incorporated to integrate deep semantic features with shallow geometric details. Furthermore, we introduce two synergistic loss functions to optimize the feature space: (1) an azimuth difference feature consistency loss, which utilizes contrastive learning to enforce intra-class feature similarity across large angular variations; (2) a dynamic angular margin loss, which adjusts classification boundaries based on the equivalent number of looks (ENL) to rigorously constrain images corrupted by speckle noise. Extensive experiments on the MSTAR and SARSIM datasets demonstrate that HAAFENet achieves excellent performance, exhibiting superior robustness and discriminability under both standard and diverse extended operating condition.

item.title: MicroDETR: DETR with frequency-spatial aware and cross-scale fusion for tiny object detection
item.dc:creator: Yanxia Lv
Guangwen Dong
Xin Song
item.description: Tiny object detection is extensively utilized across a variety of applications, such as unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) aerial imagery, marine rescue operations, industrial defect inspection, and medical imaging. However, it is particularly vulnerable to localized, coupled, and frequency-inconsistent degradations, including halos, reflections, and noise, which adversely affect localization accuracy and result in an increased incidence of missed detections. To address the insufficient response to local high-frequency details observed in existing end-to-end Transformer-based detectors for tiny object scenarios, we propose a novel detection model named MicroDETR. Initially, we introduce the Local–Global and Frequency–Spatial Interaction (LGFSI) module, which is designed to facilitate global-local interaction and cross-domain representations across both frequency and spatial domains. This module effectively reduces interference caused by halos, reflections, and noise while preserving fine-grained image details. Subsequently, we present the Tiny Object–Cross-Scale Feature Fusion (TO-CCFF) module, which integrates low-level semantic features from the backbone network and constructs a deeper feature pyramid to achieve cross-scale feature fusion. This approach enhances the feature representation capacity for tiny objects. Experimental evaluations on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that MicroDETR surpasses existing state-of-the-art models. Published: 2026-04-16T06:59:19+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.811 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yanxia Lv; Guangwen Dong; Xin Song

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113747

Relevance: 0.811 (must_read)

Tiny object detection is extensively utilized across a variety of applications, such as unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) aerial imagery, marine rescue operations, industrial defect inspection, and medical imaging. However, it is particularly vulnerable to localized, coupled, and frequency-inconsistent degradations, including halos, reflections, and noise, which adversely affect localization accuracy and result in an increased incidence of missed detections. To address the insufficient response to local high-frequency details observed in existing end-to-end Transformer-based detectors for tiny object scenarios, we propose a novel detection model named MicroDETR. Initially, we introduce the Local–Global and Frequency–Spatial Interaction (LGFSI) module, which is designed to facilitate global-local interaction and cross-domain representations across both frequency and spatial domains. This module effectively reduces interference caused by halos, reflections, and noise while preserving fine-grained image details. Subsequently, we present the Tiny Object–Cross-Scale Feature Fusion (TO-CCFF) module, which integrates low-level semantic features from the backbone network and constructs a deeper feature pyramid to achieve cross-scale feature fusion. This approach enhances the feature representation capacity for tiny objects. Experimental evaluations on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that MicroDETR surpasses existing state-of-the-art models.

item.title: Medical Multimodal Large Language Models: A Survey
item.dc:creator: Hanguang Xiao
Ningzhi Hui
Yong Xu
Zhipeng Li
Jincheng Peng
item.description: In recent years, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have gradually given rise to medical multimodal large language models (Medical MLLMs) through the integration of multimodal data such as clinical reports, medical images, physiological signals, and doctor–patient conversations. This progress has substantially improved the efficiency and quality of clinical question answering. Given the rapid development of this field and its broad clinical potential, this survey presents a systematic review of the core tasks, fundamental principles, methodological innovations, and future research directions of Medical MLLMs. Specifically, this survey first outlines the core medical tasks addressed by Medical MLLMs. It then dissects the three key modules of Medical MLLMs and, through a fine-grained vector-level analysis, explains how input feature vectors are processed and propagated across these modules. Subsequently, existing medical datasets are systematically categorized according to different stages of model training to help researchers identify relevant resources more efficiently. Furthermore, this survey elaborates on the training and evaluation methods of Medical MLLMs and discusses advanced strategies for activating their reasoning capabilities. Finally, it summarizes their practical applications in medical scenarios and highlights the critical challenges and future directions. This survey aims to provide a comprehensive reference for researchers in the field of Medical MLLMs and to promote the adaptation of Medical MLLMs to increasingly complex and diverse medical tasks. Published: 2026-04-16T15:47:10+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.810 (must_read)

This survey aims to provide a comprehensive reference for researchers in the field of Medical MLLMs and to promote the adaptation of Medical MLLMs to increasingly complex and diverse medical tasks.

item.title: RSUniVLM: A unified vision-language model for remote sensing via Granularity-oriented MoE
item.dc:creator: Xu Liu
Zhouhui Lian
item.description: Remote Sensing Vision-Language Models (RS VLMs) have made much progress in the tasks of remote sensing image comprehension. While performing well in multi-modal reasoning and multi-turn conversations, the existing models lack pixel-level understanding and struggle with multi-image inputs. In this paper, we propose RSUniVLM, a unified RS VLM designed for comprehensive vision understanding across multiple granularity, including image-level, region-level and pixel-level tasks. RSUniVLM is also capable of multi-image analysis, with strong performance in tasks such as change detection and change captioning. To enhance the model’s capacity for multi-level visual representation without significantly increasing its size, we introduce a novel Granularity-Oriented Mixture of Experts architecture, maintaining the model scale at approximately 1 billion parameters. We construct a large-scale RS instruction-following dataset by integrating a diverse set of existing datasets from both remote sensing and general domains. This dataset encompasses a wide range of tasks, including object localization, visual question answering, and semantic segmentation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RSUniVLM achieves state-of-the-art performance across a variety of RS tasks, validating its effectiveness and versatility. Published: 2026-04-15T07:11:38+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.807 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xu Liu; Zhouhui Lian

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113717

Relevance: 0.807 (must_read)

Remote Sensing Vision-Language Models (RS VLMs) have made much progress in the tasks of remote sensing image comprehension. While performing well in multi-modal reasoning and multi-turn conversations, the existing models lack pixel-level understanding and struggle with multi-image inputs. In this paper, we propose RSUniVLM, a unified RS VLM designed for comprehensive vision understanding across multiple granularity, including image-level, region-level and pixel-level tasks. RSUniVLM is also capable of multi-image analysis, with strong performance in tasks such as change detection and change captioning. To enhance the model’s capacity for multi-level visual representation without significantly increasing its size, we introduce a novel Granularity-Oriented Mixture of Experts architecture, maintaining the model scale at approximately 1 billion parameters. We construct a large-scale RS instruction-following dataset by integrating a diverse set of existing datasets from both remote sensing and general domains. This dataset encompasses a wide range of tasks, including object localization, visual question answering, and semantic segmentation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RSUniVLM achieves state-of-the-art performance across a variety of RS tasks, validating its effectiveness and versatility.

item.title: Accurate Absolute Scale with Pseudo Depth Constraint for 3D Sparse Construction of Monocular RGB Image
item.dc:creator: Erjie Jiao
Hengyou Wang
Pengju Zhang
Jun Wan
Yihong Wu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: 3D Sparse reconstruction and camera pose estimation from monocular sequences are inherently plagued by scale ambiguity, which prevents the recovery of the scene’s absolute scale. Existing solutions are primarily limited in two aspects: (1) traditional geometric methods often depend on additional sensors or restrictive pre-calibration, lacking general applicability; (2) learning-based approaches frequently suffer from scale biases when faced with domain shifts. To overcome these challenges, this paper introduces a unified framework that deeply integrates monocular depth estimation into a traditional reconstruction pipeline. Our method leverages the robustness of modern depth prediction networks to eliminate cumbersome prior assumptions while employing iterative geometric refinement to ensure precise scale accuracy. The proposed framework begins by estimating depth maps via RGB images. First, an absolute scale factor is initialized from the depth priors to calibrate the initial two-view reconstruction, effectively aligning the model into a metric space. Second, during incremental reconstruction, new points and poses are registered via depth-constrained triangulation, with an iterative weighting strategy to enhance stability. Third, we propose a joint bundle adjustment that harmonizes reprojection and geometric errors using an adaptive Mahalanobis distance, which implicitly optimizes the global scale and refines the scene geometry concurrently. Comprehensive evaluations on the ETH3D, TUM-RGBD, and ICL-NUIM benchmarks demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in absolute scale recovery. The results confirm that our method successfully maintains high geometric fidelity across diverse scenarios, proving its effectiveness and robustness. Published: 2026-04-16T19:55:06+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.806 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Erjie Jiao; Hengyou Wang; Pengju Zhang; Jun Wan; Yihong Wu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3684511

Relevance: 0.806 (must_read)

3D Sparse reconstruction and camera pose estimation from monocular sequences are inherently plagued by scale ambiguity, which prevents the recovery of the scene’s absolute scale. Existing solutions are primarily limited in two aspects: (1) traditional geometric methods often depend on additional sensors or restrictive pre-calibration, lacking general applicability; (2) learning-based approaches frequently suffer from scale biases when faced with domain shifts. To overcome these challenges, this paper introduces a unified framework that deeply integrates monocular depth estimation into a traditional reconstruction pipeline. Our method leverages the robustness of modern depth prediction networks to eliminate cumbersome prior assumptions while employing iterative geometric refinement to ensure precise scale accuracy. The proposed framework begins by estimating depth maps via RGB images. First, an absolute scale factor is initialized from the depth priors to calibrate the initial two-view reconstruction, effectively aligning the model into a metric space. Second, during incremental reconstruction, new points and poses are registered via depth-constrained triangulation, with an iterative weighting strategy to enhance stability. Third, we propose a joint bundle adjustment that harmonizes reprojection and geometric errors using an adaptive Mahalanobis distance, which implicitly optimizes the global scale and refines the scene geometry concurrently. Comprehensive evaluations on the ETH3D, TUM-RGBD, and ICL-NUIM benchmarks demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in absolute scale recovery. The results confirm that our method successfully maintains high geometric fidelity across diverse scenarios, proving its effectiveness and robustness.

item.title: Fusing Dynamic Scene-aware Augmentation with Adaptive Matching Reweighting for Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Jiayi Ding
Libo Liu
item.description: The mainstream detectors derived from transformer typically employ a one-to-one assignment strategy for object detection tasks. This strategy narrows the effective coverage of supervision signals in the high-dimensional feature space and significantly slows down model convergence when a single positive sample is exclusively paired with one predicted box. To address these bottlenecks, we propose an improved framework, dynamic-aware fine-grained distribution refinement detector (Dyna-FINE), which integrates two complementary modules. On the one hand, we design a dynamic scene-aware augmentation module that expands the effective training sample pool and reinforces cross-level feature interactions. For a single positive sample, multiple high-quality augmented prediction boxes incorporating scene semantics are generated, thereby expanding the effective supervision coverage radius in the feature space. On the other hand, we introduce a dynamic-aware supervision loss that dynamically assigns adaptive weights to supervision signals based on their quality. This loss selectively amplifies low-confidence yet valuable signals while penalizing high-confidence but low-value outliers, further extending supervision coverage radius from the optimization perspective. Extensive comparative experiments are conducted on ImageNet, Pascal VOC, and COCO datasets, demonstrating that Dyna-FINE outperforms mainstream transformer-based detector variants and traditional detectors on multiple key metrics. Published: 2026-04-15T07:23:03+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.805 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiayi Ding; Libo Liu

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132457

Relevance: 0.805 (must_read)

The mainstream detectors derived from transformer typically employ a one-to-one assignment strategy for object detection tasks. This strategy narrows the effective coverage of supervision signals in the high-dimensional feature space and significantly slows down model convergence when a single positive sample is exclusively paired with one predicted box. To address these bottlenecks, we propose an improved framework, dynamic-aware fine-grained distribution refinement detector (Dyna-FINE), which integrates two complementary modules. On the one hand, we design a dynamic scene-aware augmentation module that expands the effective training sample pool and reinforces cross-level feature interactions. For a single positive sample, multiple high-quality augmented prediction boxes incorporating scene semantics are generated, thereby expanding the effective supervision coverage radius in the feature space. On the other hand, we introduce a dynamic-aware supervision loss that dynamically assigns adaptive weights to supervision signals based on their quality. This loss selectively amplifies low-confidence yet valuable signals while penalizing high-confidence but low-value outliers, further extending supervision coverage radius from the optimization perspective. Extensive comparative experiments are conducted on ImageNet, Pascal VOC, and COCO datasets, demonstrating that Dyna-FINE outperforms mainstream transformer-based detector variants and traditional detectors on multiple key metrics.

item.title: Let Synthetic Data Shine: Domain Reassembly and Soft-Fusion for Single Domain Generalization
item.dc:creator: Hao Li
Yubin Xiao
Ke Liang
Mengzhu Wang
Long Lan
Kenli Li
Xinwang Liu
item.description: Single Domain Generalization (SDG) aims to train models that maintain consistent performance across diverse scenarios using data from a single source. While latent diffusion models (LDMs) show promise for augmenting limited source data, our analysis reveals that directly employing synthetic data may not only fail to provide benefits but can actually compromise performance due to substantial feature distribution discrepancies between synthetic and real target domains. To address this issue, we propose Discriminative Domain Reassembly and Soft-Fusion (DRSF), a training framework leveraging synthetic data to improve model generalization. We employ LDMs to produce diverse pseudo-target domain samples and introduce two key modules to handle distribution bias. First, Discriminative Feature Decoupling and Reassembly (DFDR) module uses entropy-guided attention to recalibrate channel-level features, suppressing synthetic noise while preserving semantic consistency. Second, Multi-pseudo-domain Soft Fusion (MDSF) module uses adversarial training with latent-space feature interpolation, creating continuous feature transitions between domains. Extensive SDG experiments on image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation demonstrate that DRSF delivers substantial performance gains with only marginal computational overhead. Notably, DRSF’s plug-and-play architecture enables seamless integration with unsupervised domain adaptation paradigms, underscoring its broad applicability to diverse, real-world domain challenges. Published: 2026-04-16T11:14:36+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.800 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hao Li; Yubin Xiao; Ke Liang; Mengzhu Wang; Long Lan; Kenli Li; Xinwang Liu

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02817-z

Relevance: 0.800 (must_read)

Single Domain Generalization (SDG) aims to train models that maintain consistent performance across diverse scenarios using data from a single source. While latent diffusion models (LDMs) show promise for augmenting limited source data, our analysis reveals that directly employing synthetic data may not only fail to provide benefits but can actually compromise performance due to substantial feature distribution discrepancies between synthetic and real target domains. To address this issue, we propose Discriminative Domain Reassembly and Soft-Fusion (DRSF), a training framework leveraging synthetic data to improve model generalization. We employ LDMs to produce diverse pseudo-target domain samples and introduce two key modules to handle distribution bias. First, Discriminative Feature Decoupling and Reassembly (DFDR) module uses entropy-guided attention to recalibrate channel-level features, suppressing synthetic noise while preserving semantic consistency. Second, Multi-pseudo-domain Soft Fusion (MDSF) module uses adversarial training with latent-space feature interpolation, creating continuous feature transitions between domains. Extensive SDG experiments on image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation demonstrate that DRSF delivers substantial performance gains with only marginal computational overhead. Notably, DRSF’s plug-and-play architecture enables seamless integration with unsupervised domain adaptation paradigms, underscoring its broad applicability to diverse, real-world domain challenges.

item.title: Do Instance Priors Help Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation?
item.dc:creator: Anurag Das
Anna Kukleva
Xinting Hu
Yuki M. Asano
Bernt Schiele
item.description: Semantic segmentation requires dense pixel-level annotations, which are costly and time-consuming to acquire. To address this, we present SeSAM, a framework that uses a foundational segmentation model, i.e. Segment Anything Model (SAM), with weak labels, including coarse masks, scribbles, and points. SAM, originally designed for instance-based segmentation, cannot be directly used for semantic segmentation tasks. In this work, we identify specific challenges faced by SAM and determine appropriate components to adapt it for class-based segmentation using weak labels. Specifically, SeSAM decomposes class masks into connected components, samples point prompts along object skeletons, selects SAM masks using weak-label coverage, and iteratively refines labels using pseudo-labels, enabling SAM-generated masks to be effectively used for semantic segmentation. Integrated with a semi-supervised learning framework, SeSAM balances ground-truth labels, SAM-based pseudo-labels, and high-confidence pseudo-labels, significantly improving segmentation quality. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and weak annotation types show that SeSAM consistently outperforms weakly supervised baselines while substantially reducing annotation cost relative to fine supervision. Published: 2026-04-13T08:29:49+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.797 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Anurag Das; Anna Kukleva; Xinting Hu; Yuki M. Asano; Bernt Schiele

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.797 (must_read)

Semantic segmentation requires dense pixel-level annotations, which are costly and time-consuming to acquire. To address this, we present SeSAM, a framework that uses a foundational segmentation model, i.e. Segment Anything Model (SAM), with weak labels, including coarse masks, scribbles, and points. SAM, originally designed for instance-based segmentation, cannot be directly used for semantic segmentation tasks. In this work, we identify specific challenges faced by SAM and determine appropriate components to adapt it for class-based segmentation using weak labels. Specifically, SeSAM decomposes class masks into connected components, samples point prompts along object skeletons, selects SAM masks using weak-label coverage, and iteratively refines labels using pseudo-labels, enabling SAM-generated masks to be effectively used for semantic segmentation. Integrated with a semi-supervised learning framework, SeSAM balances ground-truth labels, SAM-based pseudo-labels, and high-confidence pseudo-labels, significantly improving segmentation quality. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and weak annotation types show that SeSAM consistently outperforms weakly supervised baselines while substantially reducing annotation cost relative to fine supervision.

item.title: TIPSv2: Advancing Vision-Language Pretraining with Enhanced Patch-Text Alignment
item.dc:creator: Bingyi Cao
Koert Chen
Kevis-Kokitsi Maninis
Kaifeng Chen
Arjun Karpur
Ye Xia
Sahil Dua
Tanmaya Dabral
Guangxing Han
Bohyung Han
Joshua Ainslie
Alex Bewley
Mithun Jacob
René Wagner
Washington Ramos
Krzysztof Choromanski
Mojtaba Seyedhosseini
Howard Zhou
André Araujo
item.description: Recent progress in vision-language pretraining has enabled significant improvements to many downstream computer vision applications, such as classification, retrieval, segmentation and depth prediction. However, a fundamental capability that these models still struggle with is aligning dense patch representations with text embeddings of corresponding concepts. In this work, we investigate this critical issue and propose novel techniques to enhance this capability in foundational vision-language models. First, we reveal that a patch-level distillation procedure significantly boosts dense patch-text alignment -- surprisingly, the patch-text alignment of the distilled student model strongly surpasses that of the teacher model. This observation inspires us to consider modifications to pretraining recipes, leading us to propose iBOT++, an upgrade to the commonly-used iBOT masked image objective, where unmasked tokens also contribute directly to the loss. This dramatically enhances patch-text alignment of pretrained models. Additionally, to improve vision-language pretraining efficiency and effectiveness, we modify the exponential moving average setup in the learning recipe, and introduce a caption sampling strategy to benefit from synthetic captions at different granularities. Combining these components, we develop TIPSv2, a new family of image-text encoder models suitable for a wide range of downstream applications. Through comprehensive experiments on 9 tasks and 20 datasets, we demonstrate strong performance, generally on par with or better than recent vision encoder models. Code and models are released via our project page at https://gdm-tipsv2.github.io/ . Published: 2026-04-13T20:00:04+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.797 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Bingyi Cao; Koert Chen; Kevis-Kokitsi Maninis; Kaifeng Chen; Arjun Karpur; Ye Xia; Sahil Dua; Tanmaya Dabral; Guangxing Han; Bohyung Han; Joshua Ainslie; Alex Bewley; Mithun Jacob; René Wagner; Washington Ramos; Krzysztof Choromanski; Mojtaba Seyedhosseini; Howard Zhou; André Araujo

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.797 (must_read)

Recent progress in vision-language pretraining has enabled significant improvements to many downstream computer vision applications, such as classification, retrieval, segmentation and depth prediction. However, a fundamental capability that these models still struggle with is aligning dense patch representations with text embeddings of corresponding concepts. In this work, we investigate this critical issue and propose novel techniques to enhance this capability in foundational vision-language models. First, we reveal that a patch-level distillation procedure significantly boosts dense patch-text alignment -- surprisingly, the patch-text alignment of the distilled student model strongly surpasses that of the teacher model. This observation inspires us to consider modifications to pretraining recipes, leading us to propose iBOT++, an upgrade to the commonly-used iBOT masked image objective, where unmasked tokens also contribute directly to the loss. This dramatically enhances patch-text alignment of pretrained models. Additionally, to improve vision-language pretraining efficiency and effectiveness, we modify the exponential moving average setup in the learning recipe, and introduce a caption sampling strategy to benefit from synthetic captions at different granularities. Combining these components, we develop TIPSv2, a new family of image-text encoder models suitable for a wide range of downstream applications. Through comprehensive experiments on 9 tasks and 20 datasets, we demonstrate strong performance, generally on par with or better than recent vision encoder models. Code and models are released via our project page at https://gdm-tipsv2.github.io/ .

item.title: Text-guided Vision Token Reduction with Low-rank Adaptation for Efficient Visual Grounding
item.dc:creator: Liangtao Shi
Ting Liu
Jinxia Xie
Ning Li
Bineng Zhong
Richang Hong
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Transformer-based pretrained models have advanced Visual Grounding (VG) significantly, but their scaling up has caused soaring training and inference costs. While efforts adapting Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning to VG have cut training expenses to some extent, inference costs are over-looked, stemming from Transformer’s computation costs growing quadratically with input token length. To address the above challenge, we propose a text-guided token prune method based on a one-stream architecture for VG, named OneSVG. Specifically, OneSVG transfers low-rank adaptation LoRA to VG, and calculates the relevance between each vision token and text semantics in multiple stages, and gradually prunes the vision tokens with low relevance. In addition, traditional VG methods use a single [REG] token to predict bounding boxes, and the [REG] token relies on complete vision tokens during training, pruning large number of vision tokens will disrupt the spatial orientation information of images. Therefore, the prediction head in OneSVG first restores the square structure of images by padding the missing area, and then accepts complete vision tokens. Experimental results on three widely-used benchmarks demonstrate that our OneSVG achieves state-of-the-art real-time speed while maintaining the best accuracy with only 34.3% tokens. Codes and models are available at https://github.com/ltShi/OneSVG. Published: 2026-04-16T19:55:06+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.796 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Liangtao Shi; Ting Liu; Jinxia Xie; Ning Li; Bineng Zhong; Richang Hong

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3684576

Relevance: 0.796 (must_read)

Transformer-based pretrained models have advanced Visual Grounding (VG) significantly, but their scaling up has caused soaring training and inference costs. While efforts adapting Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning to VG have cut training expenses to some extent, inference costs are over-looked, stemming from Transformer’s computation costs growing quadratically with input token length. To address the above challenge, we propose a text-guided token prune method based on a one-stream architecture for VG, named OneSVG. Specifically, OneSVG transfers low-rank adaptation LoRA to VG, and calculates the relevance between each vision token and text semantics in multiple stages, and gradually prunes the vision tokens with low relevance. In addition, traditional VG methods use a single [REG] token to predict bounding boxes, and the [REG] token relies on complete vision tokens during training, pruning large number of vision tokens will disrupt the spatial orientation information of images. Therefore, the prediction head in OneSVG first restores the square structure of images by padding the missing area, and then accepts complete vision tokens. Experimental results on three widely-used benchmarks demonstrate that our OneSVG achieves state-of-the-art real-time speed while maintaining the best accuracy with only 34.3% tokens. Codes and models are available at https://github.com/ltShi/OneSVG.

item.title: Single-branch network with self-coaching for real-time semantic segmentation
item.dc:creator: Guoyu Yang
Daming Shi
Yuan Wang
Zunjin Zhao
item.description: Recent real-time semantic segmentation models have achieved remarkable performance and speed, yet certain challenges persist. Existing methods like multi-branch architectures can capture spatial details to enhance performance, but they often compromise inference speed; conversely, single-branch models such as SCTNet, though efficient, tend to overlook the learning of spatial details and still rely on third-party teacher models for performance enhancement. This paper proposes S2Net, a single-branch network with self-coaching that enhances both semantic information in low-resolution features and spatial details in high-resolution features without relying on third-party high-performance models, through the Self-Coaching Mechanism (SCM) and the Feature Alignment Module (FAM). Experimental results demonstrate that S2Net achieves an optimal balance between performance and speed on datasets like Cityscapes, delivering an impressive 77.3% mIoU and 211.2 FPS without the use of ImageNet pre-training. The code will be uploaded to https://github.com/gyyang23/ . Published: 2026-04-15T06:06:54+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guoyu Yang; Daming Shi; Yuan Wang; Zunjin Zhao

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113719

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Recent real-time semantic segmentation models have achieved remarkable performance and speed, yet certain challenges persist. Existing methods like multi-branch architectures can capture spatial details to enhance performance, but they often compromise inference speed; conversely, single-branch models such as SCTNet, though efficient, tend to overlook the learning of spatial details and still rely on third-party teacher models for performance enhancement. This paper proposes S2Net, a single-branch network with self-coaching that enhances both semantic information in low-resolution features and spatial details in high-resolution features without relying on third-party high-performance models, through the Self-Coaching Mechanism (SCM) and the Feature Alignment Module (FAM). Experimental results demonstrate that S2Net achieves an optimal balance between performance and speed on datasets like Cityscapes, delivering an impressive 77.3% mIoU and 211.2 FPS without the use of ImageNet pre-training. The code will be uploaded to https://github.com/gyyang23/ .

item.title: Boosting Visual Instruction Tuning with Self-Supervised Guidance
item.dc:creator: Sophia Sirko-Galouchenko
Monika Wysoczanska
Andrei Bursuc
Nicolas Thome
Spyros Gidaris
item.description: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) perform well on many vision-language tasks but often struggle with vision-centric problems that require fine-grained visual reasoning. Recent evidence suggests that this limitation arises not from weak visual representations, but from under-utilization of visual information during instruction tuning, where many tasks can be partially solved using language priors alone. We propose a simple and lightweight approach that augments visual instruction tuning with a small number of visually grounded self-supervised tasks expressed as natural language instructions. By reformulating classical self-supervised pretext tasks, such as rotation prediction, color matching, and cross-view correspondence, as image-instruction-response triplets, we introduce supervision that cannot be solved without relying on visual evidence. Our approach requires no human annotations, no architectural modifications, and no additional training stages. Across multiple models, training regimes, and benchmarks, injecting only a small fraction (3-10%) of such visually grounded instructions consistently improves performance on vision-centric evaluations. Our findings highlight instruction tuning with visually grounded SSL tasks as a powerful lever for improving visual reasoning in MLLMs through simple adjustments to the training data distribution. Code available at: https://github.com/sirkosophia/V-GIFT Published: 2026-04-14T16:59:53+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sophia Sirko-Galouchenko; Monika Wysoczanska; Andrei Bursuc; Nicolas Thome; Spyros Gidaris

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) perform well on many vision-language tasks but often struggle with vision-centric problems that require fine-grained visual reasoning. Recent evidence suggests that this limitation arises not from weak visual representations, but from under-utilization of visual information during instruction tuning, where many tasks can be partially solved using language priors alone. We propose a simple and lightweight approach that augments visual instruction tuning with a small number of visually grounded self-supervised tasks expressed as natural language instructions. By reformulating classical self-supervised pretext tasks, such as rotation prediction, color matching, and cross-view correspondence, as image-instruction-response triplets, we introduce supervision that cannot be solved without relying on visual evidence. Our approach requires no human annotations, no architectural modifications, and no additional training stages. Across multiple models, training regimes, and benchmarks, injecting only a small fraction (3-10%) of such visually grounded instructions consistently improves performance on vision-centric evaluations. Our findings highlight instruction tuning with visually grounded SSL tasks as a powerful lever for improving visual reasoning in MLLMs through simple adjustments to the training data distribution. Code available at: https://github.com/sirkosophia/V-GIFT

item.title: Interest-Aware Dual Alignments for Multimedia Recommendation
item.dc:creator: Meng Jian
Zhuoyang Xia
Feipeng Guo
Lifang Wu
item.description: In multimedia scenarios, multimodal data possesses rich semantic collaborative signals, which can help alleviate the interaction sparsity issue in personalized recommendation. However, such data also contains a massive amount of task-irrelevant and redundant information. The inherent modality heterogeneity and the challenge of multimodal interest representability pose significant difficulties. In this work, we propose an interest-aware dual alignments (IDA) model, which explores modality heterogeneity and multimodal representability throughout the interest modeling progression. In IDA, cross-modal in-depth alignment performs semantic-behavior contrastive learning from shallow to deep interest layers to build cross-modal connections and preserve complementary information. Besides, cross-item personalized alignment enhances user-centric interest perception by enforcing representation consistency among co-interacted items. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IDA effectively improves interest modeling by leveraging multimodal signals in a structured and user-aware manner, achieving state-of-the-art performance on real-world recommendation benchmarks. Published: 2026-04-15T06:24:19+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Meng Jian; Zhuoyang Xia; Feipeng Guo; Lifang Wu

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104376

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

In multimedia scenarios, multimodal data possesses rich semantic collaborative signals, which can help alleviate the interaction sparsity issue in personalized recommendation. However, such data also contains a massive amount of task-irrelevant and redundant information. The inherent modality heterogeneity and the challenge of multimodal interest representability pose significant difficulties. In this work, we propose an interest-aware dual alignments (IDA) model, which explores modality heterogeneity and multimodal representability throughout the interest modeling progression. In IDA, cross-modal in-depth alignment performs semantic-behavior contrastive learning from shallow to deep interest layers to build cross-modal connections and preserve complementary information. Besides, cross-item personalized alignment enhances user-centric interest perception by enforcing representation consistency among co-interacted items. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IDA effectively improves interest modeling by leveraging multimodal signals in a structured and user-aware manner, achieving state-of-the-art performance on real-world recommendation benchmarks.

item.title: Revisiting Compositionality in Dual-Encoder Vision-Language Models: The Role of Inference
item.dc:creator: Imanol Miranda
Ander Salaberria
Eneko Agirre
Gorka Azkune
item.description: Dual-encoder Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as CLIP are often characterized as bag-of-words systems due to their poor performance on compositional benchmarks. We argue that this limitation may stem less from deficient representations than from the standard inference protocol based on global cosine similarity. First, through controlled diagnostic experiments, we show that explicitly enforcing fine-grained region-segment alignment at inference dramatically improves compositional performance without updating pretrained encoders. We then introduce a lightweight transformer that learns such alignments directly from frozen patch and token embeddings. Comparing against full fine-tuning and prior end-to-end compositional training methods, we find that although these approaches improve in-domain retrieval, their gains do not consistently transfer under distribution shift. In contrast, learning localized alignment over frozen representations matches full fine-tuning on in-domain retrieval while yielding substantial improvements on controlled out-of-domain compositional benchmarks. These results identify global embedding matching as a key bottleneck in dual-encoder VLMs and highlight the importance of alignment mechanisms for robust compositional generalization. Published: 2026-04-13T14:03:18+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Imanol Miranda; Ander Salaberria; Eneko Agirre; Gorka Azkune

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Dual-encoder Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as CLIP are often characterized as bag-of-words systems due to their poor performance on compositional benchmarks. We argue that this limitation may stem less from deficient representations than from the standard inference protocol based on global cosine similarity. First, through controlled diagnostic experiments, we show that explicitly enforcing fine-grained region-segment alignment at inference dramatically improves compositional performance without updating pretrained encoders. We then introduce a lightweight transformer that learns such alignments directly from frozen patch and token embeddings. Comparing against full fine-tuning and prior end-to-end compositional training methods, we find that although these approaches improve in-domain retrieval, their gains do not consistently transfer under distribution shift. In contrast, learning localized alignment over frozen representations matches full fine-tuning on in-domain retrieval while yielding substantial improvements on controlled out-of-domain compositional benchmarks. These results identify global embedding matching as a key bottleneck in dual-encoder VLMs and highlight the importance of alignment mechanisms for robust compositional generalization.

item.title: ConvDepth: Self-supervised monocular depth estimation for autonomous driving
item.dc:creator: Wei Xiong
Song Wang
item.description: Self-supervised monocular depth estimation (MDE) has emerged as a pivotal task for autonomous driving, yet standard convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures often struggle to balance global context understanding with fine-grained boundary precision. In this paper, we propose ConvDepth, a robust and efficient framework that leverages modern convolutional design to bridge this gap. At the core of our approach is a ConvNeXt-based encoder paired with a novel multi-level detail enhancement (MLDE) module, which utilizes omni-dimensional convolutions (ODConv) to capture intricate spatial dependencies across hierarchical scales. To address depth ambiguity in textureless and reflective regions, we introduce a probabilistic disparity head (DH) that reformulates depth regression as a discrete probability distribution task. Furthermore, we propose a robust self-distillation loss ( " role="presentation"> ) to mitigate the noise inherent in self-supervised photometric signals by adaptively filtering outliers while leveraging high-resolution pseudo-labels. Extensive experiments on the KITTI, Make3D, and Cityscapes datasets demonstrate that ConvDepth significantly outperforms existing CNN-based baselines and remains highly competitive with state-of-the-art Transformer-based models. Qualitative evaluations further confirm that ConvDepth excels in delineating slender structures and complex urban geometries, offering a hardware-efficient yet high-performance solution for real-world depth perception. Published: 2026-04-15T06:15:18+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.794 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wei Xiong; Song Wang

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.133681

Relevance: 0.794 (must_read)

Self-supervised monocular depth estimation (MDE) has emerged as a pivotal task for autonomous driving, yet standard convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures often struggle to balance global context understanding with fine-grained boundary precision. In this paper, we propose ConvDepth, a robust and efficient framework that leverages modern convolutional design to bridge this gap. At the core of our approach is a ConvNeXt-based encoder paired with a novel multi-level detail enhancement (MLDE) module, which utilizes omni-dimensional convolutions (ODConv) to capture intricate spatial dependencies across hierarchical scales. To address depth ambiguity in textureless and reflective regions, we introduce a probabilistic disparity head (DH) that reformulates depth regression as a discrete probability distribution task. Furthermore, we propose a robust self-distillation loss ( " role="presentation"> ) to mitigate the noise inherent in self-supervised photometric signals by adaptively filtering outliers while leveraging high-resolution pseudo-labels. Extensive experiments on the KITTI, Make3D, and Cityscapes datasets demonstrate that ConvDepth significantly outperforms existing CNN-based baselines and remains highly competitive with state-of-the-art Transformer-based models. Qualitative evaluations further confirm that ConvDepth excels in delineating slender structures and complex urban geometries, offering a hardware-efficient yet high-performance solution for real-world depth perception.

item.title: Switch-KD: Visual-Switch Knowledge Distillation for Vision-Language Models
item.dc:creator: Haoyi Sun
Xiaoxiao Wang
Ning Mao
Qian Wang
Lifu Mu
Wen Zheng
Tao Wei
Wei Chen
item.description: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in joint vision-language understanding, but their large scale poses significant challenges for deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. Knowledge Distillation (KD) offers a viable way to improve model capabilities without increasing model size or data requirements, making deployment more efficient. However, applying KD to VLMs is challenged by modality-specific supervision: although multimodal knowledge in VLMs is fused within the language space, current methods supervise each modality separately without explicitly addressing multimodal alignment, leading to inconsistent multimodal knowledge transfer. To address this, we propose Switch-KD, a visual-switch distillation framework that unifies vision-language knowledge transfer within a shared text-probability space. Switch-KD comprises two key components: (1) Visual-Switch Distillation, which switches the student's visual outputs into the teacher's language pathway to construct cross-modal probabilistic references for implicit visual knowledge transfer; and (2) Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss, which adaptively aligns informative probability regions while preserving the distributional structures of teacher and student through bidirectional supervision. Guided by Switch-KD, a 0.5B TinyLLaVA effectively distills rich multimodal knowledge from its 3B teacher, yielding an average improvement of 3.6 points across 10 multimodal benchmarks without any architectural modification. Published: 2026-04-16T05:13:57+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Haoyi Sun; Xiaoxiao Wang; Ning Mao; Qian Wang; Lifu Mu; Wen Zheng; Tao Wei; Wei Chen

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in joint vision-language understanding, but their large scale poses significant challenges for deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. Knowledge Distillation (KD) offers a viable way to improve model capabilities without increasing model size or data requirements, making deployment more efficient. However, applying KD to VLMs is challenged by modality-specific supervision: although multimodal knowledge in VLMs is fused within the language space, current methods supervise each modality separately without explicitly addressing multimodal alignment, leading to inconsistent multimodal knowledge transfer. To address this, we propose Switch-KD, a visual-switch distillation framework that unifies vision-language knowledge transfer within a shared text-probability space. Switch-KD comprises two key components: (1) Visual-Switch Distillation, which switches the student's visual outputs into the teacher's language pathway to construct cross-modal probabilistic references for implicit visual knowledge transfer; and (2) Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss, which adaptively aligns informative probability regions while preserving the distributional structures of teacher and student through bidirectional supervision. Guided by Switch-KD, a 0.5B TinyLLaVA effectively distills rich multimodal knowledge from its 3B teacher, yielding an average improvement of 3.6 points across 10 multimodal benchmarks without any architectural modification.

item.title: From Alignment to Prediction: A Study of Self-Supervised Learning and Predictive Representation Learning
item.dc:creator: Mintu Dutta
Ritesh Vyas
Mohendra Roy
item.description: Self-supervised learning has emerged as a major technique for the task of learning from unlabeled data, where the current methods mostly revolve around alignment of representations and input recon struction. Although such approaches have demonstrated excellent performance in practice, their scope remains mostly confined to learning from observed data and does not provide much help in terms of a learning structure that is predictive of the data distribution. In this paper, we study some of the recent developments in the realm of self-supervised learning. We define a new category called Predictive Representation Learning (PRL), which revolves around the latent prediction of unobserved components of data based on the observation. We propose a common taxonomy that classifies PRL along with alignment and reconstruction-based learning approaches. Furthermore, we argue that Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture(JEPA) can be considered as an exemplary member of this new paradigm. We further discuss theoretical perspectives and open challenges, highlighting predictive representation learning as a promising direction for future self-supervised learning research. In this study, we implemented Bootstrap Your Own Latent (BYOL), Masked Autoencoders (MAE), and Image-JEPA (I-JEPA) for comparative analysis. The results indicate that MAE achieves perfect similarity of 1.00, but exhibits relatively weak robustness of 0.55. In contrast, BYOL and I-JEPA attain accuracies of 0.98 and 0.95, with robustness scores of 0.75 and 0.78, respectively. Published: 2026-04-15T06:04:45+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.790 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mintu Dutta; Ritesh Vyas; Mohendra Roy

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.790 (must_read)

Self-supervised learning has emerged as a major technique for the task of learning from unlabeled data, where the current methods mostly revolve around alignment of representations and input recon struction. Although such approaches have demonstrated excellent performance in practice, their scope remains mostly confined to learning from observed data and does not provide much help in terms of a learning structure that is predictive of the data distribution. In this paper, we study some of the recent developments in the realm of self-supervised learning. We define a new category called Predictive Representation Learning (PRL), which revolves around the latent prediction of unobserved components of data based on the observation. We propose a common taxonomy that classifies PRL along with alignment and reconstruction-based learning approaches. Furthermore, we argue that Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture(JEPA) can be considered as an exemplary member of this new paradigm. We further discuss theoretical perspectives and open challenges, highlighting predictive representation learning as a promising direction for future self-supervised learning research. In this study, we implemented Bootstrap Your Own Latent (BYOL), Masked Autoencoders (MAE), and Image-JEPA (I-JEPA) for comparative analysis. The results indicate that MAE achieves perfect similarity of 1.00, but exhibits relatively weak robustness of 0.55. In contrast, BYOL and I-JEPA attain accuracies of 0.98 and 0.95, with robustness scores of 0.75 and 0.78, respectively.

item.title: Efficient Dynamic Spatial-Focused Attention Model for Spatiotemporal Traffic Prediction
item.dc:creator: Nan Ouyang
Wenkang Wan
Lei Ao
Shehui Bu
Xiaojiang Ren
Xin He
Kai Sheng
item.description: Accurate road traffic prediction is crucial for improving transportation efficiency and reducing carbon emissions. However, existing Transformer-based spatio-temporal (ST) models incur high computational cost by uniformly attending to all spatial nodes, despite the strong redundancy and low-entropy sensors commonly present in large traffic networks. To address this issue, we propose DSFA-former, a Transformer-based ST forecasting framework built around a Dynamic Spatial-Focused Attention (DSF-Attention) mechanism. DSF-Attention dynamically screens informative spatial nodes via learned projections, performs attention on a reduced node set, and aggregates the attended features into a global spatial context that is broadcast back to all nodes. This design suppresses low-entropy or noisy nodes while explicitly reducing attention complexity without sacrificing critical ST dependencies. In addition, DSFA-former integrates an inverted feature embedding strategy and a learnable Gaussian-kernel-enhanced graph attention module to model temporal dynamics and structured spatial interactions. Experiments on seven real-world traffic datasets show that DSFA-former achieves advanced predictive accuracy, with average error reductions of 9.9%, 11.1%, and 8.5% on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, and BJTT, respectively. Moreover, ablation studies across four datasets show that DSF-Attention substantially improves computational efficiency, reducing training time by 51.18% and GPU memory consumption by 44.21% compared with standard attention mechanisms under identical computational environments, while simultaneously improving prediction accuracy. Published: 2026-04-16T15:47:10+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.790 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Nan Ouyang; Wenkang Wan; Lei Ao; Shehui Bu; Xiaojiang Ren; Xin He; Kai Sheng

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104380

Relevance: 0.790 (must_read)

Accurate road traffic prediction is crucial for improving transportation efficiency and reducing carbon emissions. However, existing Transformer-based spatio-temporal (ST) models incur high computational cost by uniformly attending to all spatial nodes, despite the strong redundancy and low-entropy sensors commonly present in large traffic networks. To address this issue, we propose DSFA-former, a Transformer-based ST forecasting framework built around a Dynamic Spatial-Focused Attention (DSF-Attention) mechanism. DSF-Attention dynamically screens informative spatial nodes via learned projections, performs attention on a reduced node set, and aggregates the attended features into a global spatial context that is broadcast back to all nodes. This design suppresses low-entropy or noisy nodes while explicitly reducing attention complexity without sacrificing critical ST dependencies. In addition, DSFA-former integrates an inverted feature embedding strategy and a learnable Gaussian-kernel-enhanced graph attention module to model temporal dynamics and structured spatial interactions. Experiments on seven real-world traffic datasets show that DSFA-former achieves advanced predictive accuracy, with average error reductions of 9.9%, 11.1%, and 8.5% on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, and BJTT, respectively. Moreover, ablation studies across four datasets show that DSF-Attention substantially improves computational efficiency, reducing training time by 51.18% and GPU memory consumption by 44.21% compared with standard attention mechanisms under identical computational environments, while simultaneously improving prediction accuracy.

item.title: Cross-Attentive Multiview Fusion of Vision-Language Embeddings
item.dc:creator: Tomas Berriel Martins
Martin R. Oswald
Javier Civera
item.description: Vision-language models have been key to the development of open-vocabulary 2D semantic segmentation. Lifting these models from 2D images to 3D scenes, however, remains a challenging problem. Existing approaches typically back-project and average 2D descriptors across views, or heuristically select a single representative one, often resulting in suboptimal 3D representations. In this work, we introduce a novel multiview transformer architecture that cross-attends across vision-language descriptors from multiple viewpoints and fuses them into a unified per-3D-instance embedding. As a second contribution, we leverage multiview consistency as a self-supervision signal for this fusion, which significantly improves performance when added to a standard supervised target-class loss. Our Cross-Attentive Multiview Fusion, which we denote with its acronym CAMFusion, not only consistently outperforms naive averaging or single-view descriptor selection, but also achieves state-of-the-art results on 3D semantic and instance classification benchmarks, including zero-shot evaluations on out-of-domain datasets. Published: 2026-04-14T10:25:32+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.789 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tomas Berriel Martins; Martin R. Oswald; Javier Civera

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.789 (must_read)

Vision-language models have been key to the development of open-vocabulary 2D semantic segmentation. Lifting these models from 2D images to 3D scenes, however, remains a challenging problem. Existing approaches typically back-project and average 2D descriptors across views, or heuristically select a single representative one, often resulting in suboptimal 3D representations. In this work, we introduce a novel multiview transformer architecture that cross-attends across vision-language descriptors from multiple viewpoints and fuses them into a unified per-3D-instance embedding. As a second contribution, we leverage multiview consistency as a self-supervision signal for this fusion, which significantly improves performance when added to a standard supervised target-class loss. Our Cross-Attentive Multiview Fusion, which we denote with its acronym CAMFusion, not only consistently outperforms naive averaging or single-view descriptor selection, but also achieves state-of-the-art results on 3D semantic and instance classification benchmarks, including zero-shot evaluations on out-of-domain datasets.

item.title: Improving Out-of-Distribution Generalization on Point Clouds with Cross-Domain Adversarial Distillation
item.dc:creator: Bowei Xing
Xianghua Ying
Ruibin Wang
item.description: Point cloud is an important data format to represent 3D objects and real world scenes, which is widely used in many fields such as virtual reality and autonomous driving. In recent years, various methods have been proposed for point cloud recognition and achieved great performance. However, since these methods are always under the i.i.d. assumption, the trained model inevitably suffers drastic performance degradation when evaluated on out-of-distribution (OOD) data. To address the above problem, in this paper, we propose a framework named cross-domain adversarial distillation, with the goal of learning a model that generalizes well on OOD point clouds. Concretely, we initiate by dynamically generating adversarial point cloud examples that deviate from original domain and exhibit diversification, so as to progressively broaden the training distribution. Subsequently, we implement a bidirectional distillation scheme to ensure semantic consistency between the generated novel-domain example and source domain example, which facilitates the exchange of cross-domain knowledge through teacher-student cooperation. By iteratively alternating between the above processes, the model is encouraged to learn more generalizable representations when encountering point clouds from novel distributions, leading to improved performance on the unseen target domain. We carry experiments on two typical datasets, ModelNet40-C for corrupted point clouds and Sim-to-Real for real-world point clouds, implemented with different 3D recognition models. The experimental results show that our method achieves better performance on these two benchmarks, which demonstrate the effectiveness of our method for improving point cloud OOD generalization. Published: 2026-04-16T19:53:54+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.789 (must_read)
